title: Conversation Mechanics — Hesitation, Aizuchi & Discourse Markers

meta.focus: Master the sounds that hold the floor (えーと, あのう, うーん), active-listening signals (なるほど, へえ, そうですね), casual fillers (なんか, まあ), and discourse restructuring markers (じつは, つまり).

sections.title: Conversation Mechanics — Hesitation, Aizuchi & Discourse Markers
sections.summary: You have grammar. Now you need conversation survival: the sounds that hold the floor and the sounds that show you're listening. Real Japanese conversation is not a sequence of complete sentences — it is a flow of signals, pauses, and short responses that keep both speakers connected and the exchange moving.
sections.whyItMatters: Japanese speakers use hesitation sounds and aizuchi constantly. Getting these right is the difference between sounding like a textbook and sounding like a real conversation partner. They also signal to your listener that you are still talking — or that you're genuinely engaged when it's their turn.
youWillLearn: Floor-holding sounds (えーと, えっと, あのう, うーん) — how they signal 'I'm still speaking'
さあ — how the same sound expresses both uncertainty and commencement
うん vs うーん — how length and tone alone change the meaning completely
相槌 (aizuchi) — the listener's toolkit: なるほど, へえ, ほんとうに, そうそう, and more
Casual fillers なんか and まあ — what they add to speech and when not to use them
Discourse restructuring markers じつは (actually) and つまり (in other words)

pattern.label: FLOOR-HOLDER
pattern.text: えーと / あのう / うーん

sections.meaning: I'm still speaking — please don't take the floor
sections.explanation: In Japanese conversation, silence signals that you have finished your turn and are yielding the floor to your listener. This is different from many other languages where a pause is neutral. Floor-holding sounds like えーと, あのう, and うーん fill that silence with an audible signal: 'I am still processing — the floor is still mine.' Without them, a pause reads as an invitation for the other person to speak.
notes: えーと (also えっと — slightly shorter and more casual) is the most neutral floor-holder. Use it while searching for a word or deciding what to say.
あのう is slightly hesitant and apologetic in tone. It often opens a request or a topic the speaker feels uncertain about raising.
うーん is used when genuinely unsure or weighing options. Its long, falling tone signals active deliberation rather than word-searching.
All three are social tools, not verbal filler to be avoided. Japanese speakers use them naturally and frequently — learning to use them makes you sound like a real conversation partner.
Register: all three are appropriate in casual to semi-formal speech. In very formal contexts, speakers reduce but do not eliminate them.

parts.gloss: what was it again? (casual memory check)

examples.en: Um… what was his name again?
examples.breakdown: えーと buys thinking time while the speaker searches their memory. The floor remains theirs throughout the pause.

examples.en: Um… would it be okay if I asked you something?
examples.breakdown: あのう introduces a hesitant request. Its apologetic tone softens the ask before the main question even arrives.
うーん with a long falling tone signals genuine deliberation. The speaker is still holding the floor while thinking through the problem.

sections.meaning: I don't know / Well then, let's go — context determines which
sections.explanation: さあ is unique: the same sound carries two completely different functions depending on what follows and the speaker's intonation. As an uncertainty marker, さあ expresses 'I'm not sure / I really don't know' — often with a falling or drawn-out tone. As a commencement marker, さあ means 'well then / right then / let's go' — used to launch into action with a rising, energetic tone. Context almost always makes the meaning clear.
notes: Uncertainty さあ: 「さあ、よくわかりません」(Hmm, I really don't know) — the speaker genuinely cannot answer. Often followed by trailing off or わかりません.
Commencement さあ: 「さあ、行きましょう！」(Right then, let's go!) — the speaker is initiating movement or activity. Rising, energetic tone.
The two functions feel completely distinct in context. Pay attention to what follows: a statement of not-knowing (uncertainty) vs an invitation to act (commencement).
さあ as uncertainty is neutral; さあ as commencement is energetic and common across all registers.

examples.breakdown: さあ signals the speaker genuinely doesn't know. The falling tone and わかりません confirm uncertainty.
さあ launches into action. The rising, energetic tone and the volitional verb confirm commencement, not uncertainty.

sections.title: Hesitation Sounds — Quick Reference
sections.description: All six forms at a glance. The Nuance column is the key — that is what separates natural from mechanical use.

rows.label: えーと
cells: Searching for a word or information; neutral floor-hold

rows.label: えっと
cells: Shorter, slightly more casual than えーと; same floor-holding function

rows.label: あのう
cells: Hesitant opener; slightly apologetic; softens a request or a difficult topic

rows.label: うーん
cells: Genuine deliberation; weighing options; long falling tone

rows.label: さあ (uncertainty)
cells: Speaker genuinely doesn't know; often precedes わかりません

rows.label: さあ (commencement)
cells: Launches into action; rising tone; initiates movement or a task

notes: えーと and えっと are interchangeable in meaning; えっと feels slightly more clipped and casual.
All hesitation sounds can be lengthened (えーーーと, うーーーん) to signal even more thinking time.
In very formal speech such as a job interview, reduce but do not eliminate these sounds — they remain natural.

sections.title: うん vs うーん — Length Carries the Meaning

items.label: うん
points: Short, flat affirmative — means 'yes / yeah / mm-hmm'
Agreement or acknowledgment; the plain casual equivalent of はい
Delivered with a single short rising-falling pitch: 'un'
Can be doubled (うんうん) to mean 'yeah yeah — I hear you, keep going'

items.label: うーん
points: Long, falling deliberative sound — means 'hmm… / well…'
Signals thinking, weighing, or hesitating — NOT affirmative
Delivered with a long falling or drawn-out pitch: 'uuuun'
Signals uncertainty or that the speaker is still processing

sections.tip: The length and tone of the vowel carries ALL the meaning. うん (short, flat) = yes. うーん (long, falling) = I'm still thinking. Confusing them tells your listener the opposite of what you mean.

sections.meaning: Active-listening responses that keep conversation flowing
sections.explanation: 相槌 (aizuchi) are the short responses a listener makes while the speaker is still talking. Unlike interruptions, aizuchi are expected and welcomed — they tell the speaker 'I'm with you, keep going.' Japanese conversation has a much higher density of aizuchi than many other languages. Staying completely silent while listening can feel cold or disengaged; the right aizuchi feels warm and attentive.
notes: Aizuchi are not just for the ends of sentences — you can offer them mid-explanation when the speaker naturally pauses.
The choice of aizuchi signals how you feel about what you just heard: surprise (へえ！), understanding (なるほど), agreement (そうそう), or seeking confirmation (ほんとうに？).
Stacking aizuchi: Japanese speakers often stack two or three: 「そうですね、なるほど」or 「うんうん、そうそう」. This signals deep engagement.
Register matters: うん/うんうん is strictly casual. はい/ええ is polite. そうですね is polite-to-neutral. なるほど/へえ work across most registers. そうそう is casual.
Over-using a single aizuchi sounds robotic. Vary them naturally based on what you genuinely feel about what you heard.

examples.context: Listener shows genuine understanding with なるほど

parts.gloss: I see / that makes sense (comprehension aizuchi)
so that's how it is (casual realization)

examples.en: Ah, I see — so that's how it works.
examples.breakdown: なるほど signals genuine insight — something clicked for the listener. It is more substantive than a simple 'yes.'

examples.context: Listener shows surprise with へえ, then seeks confirmation with ほんとうに
examples.breakdown: へえ expresses genuine surprise or interest. Pairing it with ほんとうに？ amplifies the reaction and invites the speaker to confirm.

examples.context: Listener enthusiastically agrees with そうそう

parts.gloss: yes, exactly! / right, right! (enthusiastic casual agreement)

examples.breakdown: そうそう is casual and enthusiastic. It signals the listener is fully on board and energized by the shared understanding.

sections.title: Aizuchi Toolkit
sections.description: A listener's complete toolkit. The 'When to use' column is the key — each aizuchi carries a specific signal about how you feel.

rows.label: うん / うんうん
cells: Casual ongoing acknowledgment; うんうん (repeated) = 'keep going, I'm with you'

rows.label: はい / ええ
cells: Polite acknowledgment; ええ is softer than はい; both signal active listening

rows.label: そうですね
cells: Polite agreement or shared recognition; does not express surprise

rows.label: なるほど
cells: When something clicks — after an explanation, when the listener genuinely understands

rows.label: へえ
cells: When information is surprising or new and interesting to the listener

rows.label: ほんとうに
cells: Invites confirmation of surprising information; rising intonation

rows.label: そうそう
cells: Enthusiastic agreement; the listener feels the speaker has nailed it

notes: そうだね (casual equivalent of そうですね) is common between close friends — it uses the plain copula だ.
In business Japanese, overuse of うん or そうそう can seem too casual. Prefer はい, ええ, and なるほど in formal settings.
ほんとうに is always written in hiragana in this curriculum — never as 本当に.

sections.meaning: Casual softeners — 'kind of / like' (なんか) and 'well / sort of' (まあ)
sections.explanation: なんか and まあ are casual fillers that soften or hedge a statement. They signal that the speaker is not making a precise or absolute claim — adding a conversational, approachable tone. Both are extremely common in everyday speech among friends. Both carry register restrictions that learners must respect.
notes: なんか literally means 'something' (何か), but as a filler it functions like English 'like' or 'kind of'. It hedges what follows: 「なんか、むずかしいな」= 'Kind of difficult, you know.' It can appear at the start of a sentence or mid-utterance.
REGISTER WARNING for なんか: as a filler, なんか is strongly casual. Using it with superiors, in formal settings, or with strangers sounds sloppy or rude. Reserve it for friends and informal contexts.

まあ means 'well / sort of / I suppose'. It signals moderate acceptance or a nuanced position: 「まあ、いいか」= 'Well, I suppose it's fine.' It is slightly less casual than なんか and can appear in semi-formal contexts.
Both appear frequently in native speech because they soften the directness of Japanese statements — mastering them makes you sound natural; misusing them in the wrong register sounds rude.

examples.context: なんか as a casual sentence-opening filler
examples.en: I can't really explain it well, but…
examples.breakdown: なんか softens the admission of struggling to articulate. It adds an approachable, self-deprecating tone rather than a blunt 'I can't explain it.'

examples.en: Well, I suppose there's nothing to be done.
examples.breakdown: まあ introduces calm, resigned acceptance. It is less absolute than しかたない alone — the speaker is making peace with the situation.

sections.meaning: Discourse markers that reframe or restructure what has been said
sections.explanation: じつは and つまり are discourse restructuring markers — they signal that the conversation is about to shift or be reframed. じつは (actually / to tell the truth) introduces information that contrasts with what the listener assumed. つまり (in other words / that is to say) summarizes or restates what was already said in clearer terms. Both are essential for natural explanatory speech.
notes: じつは appears at the start of a clause. It signals: 'What I am about to say is the real situation, which may differ from what you assumed.' Common in confessions, surprising reveals, and topic shifts.
IMPORTANT: always write じつは in hiragana. The kanji 実 is not in the taught set — do not write 実は.
つまり also appears at the start of a clause. It signals: 'Let me sum that up / here is the core point.' Use it to clarify, conclude, or restate after an explanation.
じつは vs つまり: じつは introduces new (possibly surprising) information; つまり summarizes or restates existing information. They are not interchangeable.
Both work in casual and polite registers — they are discourse-level tools, not register-specific vocabulary.

examples.context: じつは — revealing something the listener didn't know

parts.gloss: actually / to tell you the truth (discourse marker)

examples.breakdown: じつは sets up a reveal — the speaker is sharing something the listener didn't know and may not have expected.

examples.context: つまり — summarizing what was just explained

parts.gloss: in other words / to sum up (discourse marker)
is that what you mean? (casual clarification)

examples.en: In other words, you're saying there's no time?
examples.breakdown: つまり invites a restatement or confirmation of what was just explained. The listener uses it to summarize back what they heard.

examples.context: りきぞ holds the floor with えーと, reveals with じつは, lists reasons with し, and uses のに for frustration

parts.gloss: even though (のに — frustrating, unexpected contrast)

examples.en: Um… actually, next week work is busy, and even though it's tough, I can't take a break.
examples.note: えーと holds the floor while りきぞ collects his thoughts. じつは signals a reveal the listener didn't know. し lists two reasons (busy + difficult). のに expresses the frustrating gap between expectation and reality — of course if it's difficult, you should be able to rest.

examples.context: けん responds with aizuchi chain — へえ, そうですね — then shifts to action with さあ and summarizes with つまり

parts.gloss: well then / right then (commencement — shifting to action)

examples.en: Oh wow! I see. Well then — in other words, we have no choice but to meet this weekend, huh?
examples.note: へえ shows genuine surprise. そうですね confirms shared understanding. さあ shifts to action-planning mode. つまり draws the logical conclusion from what was just explained.

sections.title: Planning the Weekend
sections.context: りきぞ and けん are friends chatting after class. Casual register throughout — plain forms, だ copula, and casual aizuchi.

lines.en: Hey, are you free this weekend?

lines.jp: えーと、じつは来週は仕事がいそがしいし、仕事が多いのに休めないから、週まつは外に出たい。
lines.en: Um… actually, next week work is busy and there's a lot of it — even though I can't rest, I want to get out this weekend.

lines.jp: へえ！ほんとうに？つかれても、出たいよ。
lines.en: Wow! Really? Even though you're tired, you want to get out?

lines.jp: そうそう！まあ、なんか、家にいるのもいいけど、そとに出たいし。
lines.en: Exactly! Well, I mean, staying home is fine too, but I also want to get outside.

lines.jp: なるほど。さあ、映画を見てから、ご飯を食べようか？
lines.en: I see! Right then — how about after watching a movie, we eat somewhere?

lines.jp: うん、いいね！あのう、つまり、なんか見たい映画があるけど、どこで食べるかはまだわからない。
lines.en: Yeah, sounds great! Um, well — like, there's a movie I want to see, but I still don't know where to eat.

lines.jp: そうですね。映画を見ながら、どこで食べるか話そう。
lines.en: Right. Let's talk about where to eat while we watch the movie.

sections.title: Still Speaking or Yielding the Floor?
sections.instructions: Read speaker A's utterance. Is A still holding the floor (true), or have they finished their turn and yielded it (false)?

items.sentence: A: えーと、来週の予定は… (A pauses with a rising, incomplete tone)
items.explanation: えーと signals A is still searching for words. The trailing 「…」 with a rising, incomplete tone means A has not finished. The listener should wait — taking the floor here would feel like an interruption.

items.sentence: A: うーん。(long, falling tone, then several seconds of complete silence)
items.explanation: うーん (long, falling) signals deliberation — A is still thinking. Despite the pause, the drawn-out falling vowel signals A has not yielded the floor. A skilled listener may offer a gentle aizuchi after a longer wait, but should not begin a new topic.

items.sentence: A: なるほど、そういうことですね。(clear falling intonation, sentence-final)
items.explanation: なるほど + そういうことですね is a complete aizuchi response ending with sentence-final falling intonation. A has finished their listener turn. The floor is open — B may now speak as the primary speaker.

items.sentence: A: あのう、ちょっと聞いてもいいですか。(complete question, falling sentence-final intonation)
items.explanation: Even though あのう began the utterance as a hesitation opener, the sentence 「ちょっと聞いてもいいですか」is grammatically complete with sentence-final falling intonation. A has finished asking their question. B now holds the floor to respond.

sections.title: Practice: Hesitation, Aizuchi & Discourse
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: Which sound signals 'I am still thinking — please don't take the floor yet'?
items.explanation: えーと is a floor-holding hesitation sound — it signals the speaker is still processing and has not finished their turn. なるほど, そうですね, and そうそう are all aizuchi — listener responses that do not hold the floor; they respond to what the speaker has said.

items.q: りきぞ is reporting to his boss about a project. Which of the following is NOT appropriate to use?
items.explanation: なんか as a filler is strongly casual — using it with a superior or in a formal setting sounds sloppy or disrespectful. えーと, あのう, and うーん are all acceptable in semi-formal speech. In formal contexts their frequency should be reduced, but they remain natural.

items.q: けん says 「うーん」with a long falling tone in response to a question. What does this mean?
choices: I'm not sure — I'm thinking about it.
items.answer: I'm not sure — I'm thinking about it.
items.explanation: うーん (long, falling tone) signals deliberation or uncertainty — NOT agreement. It is the opposite of うん (short, flat), which means 'yes / yeah.' The length and tone carry all the meaning: short + flat = yes; long + falling = I'm still thinking.

items.q: Which discourse marker would you use to introduce a surprising fact the listener didn't know?
items.explanation: じつは (actually / to tell the truth) introduces information that may contrast with the listener's assumptions — perfect for a surprising reveal. つまり summarizes or restates in other words. なるほど and そうそう are aizuchi (listener responses), not speaker-initiated discourse markers.

items.q: A speaker gives a long explanation. The listener wants to show they understood and could summarize it. Which aizuchi fits best?
items.explanation: なるほど means 'I see / that makes sense' — it signals genuine comprehension after an explanation, as if something clicked. へえ expresses surprise at new information, not deep comprehension. うん is a simple casual acknowledgment. そうそう is enthusiastic agreement, not the comprehension signal that fits after a long explanation.
